title: Shakshuka
subtitle: Poached eggs in spiced tomato and pepper sauce
excerpt: Shakshuka is poached eggs cooked directly in a thick, spiced tomato and pepper sauce. The eggs go in raw, the pan goes on a low heat with a lid, and the whites set into the sauce while the yolks stay runny. Serve straight from the pan with flatbread or crusty bread to scoop.
sourceNotes:
• Yotam Ottolenghi and Sami Tamimi, *Jerusalem* (2012). Principal reference for the sauce proportions and the egg-poaching technique.
• Claudia Roden, *A New Book of Middle Eastern Food* (1985). Cross-reference for the North African origin and the cumin and paprika combination.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The tomato sauce can be made up to 2 days ahead and refrigerated. Reheat to a simmer before adding the eggs.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dish of eggs poached in a spiced tomato and pepper sauce, originating in North Africa and eaten widely across the Middle East. The name comes from the Arabic for 'all mixed up'. Traditionally cooked and served in the pan.

content.text: works because the tomato sauce does two things at once: it flavours the eggs as they cook and provides the steam that sets the whites from above. The key is a thick, fully reduced sauce before the eggs go in — if the sauce is too wet, the eggs poach in liquid rather than setting into the sauce, and the whites take too long and become rubbery while the yolks overcook.

The pan size matters. A 26 cm frying pan gives enough room for 6 eggs with the sauce. A smaller pan crowds the eggs and makes it hard to get a lid that seals well enough. Serve with flatbread, pitta, or crusty sourdough.
A wide, lidded frying pan is essential. If the pan has no lid, a large baking sheet set over the top works.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over a medium heat. Add {{onion}} sliced onion and {{pepper-red}} of sliced red pepper. Cook for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until completely soft. Add {{garlic}} of garlic, {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes. Stir for 1 minute.

Pour in {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes. Season with salt, stir well, and simmer over a medium heat for 12 to 15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce is thick and no longer watery. It should hold a spoon-trail for a few seconds.

Make 6 small wells in the sauce using the back of a spoon. Crack {{eggs}} of eggs into the wells, one per well. Season each egg lightly with salt. Cover the pan with a lid and cook over a low heat for 6 to 8 minutes until the whites are just set and the yolks are still runny. Check at 6 minutes — egg size and pan temperature vary, and overcooked yolks cannot be undone.
Scatter with crumbled feta and chopped parsley if using, and serve straight from the pan.

items.symptom: Egg whites are set but yolks are hard
items.cause: The heat was too high or the lid trapped too much steam.
items.fix: Keep the heat at the lowest setting once the eggs are in. Check at 5 minutes: the whites should be just opaque at the edges, with the yolk still visibly runny in the centre.

items.symptom: Egg whites are still raw after 10 minutes
items.cause: The sauce was too cold when the eggs went in, or there was not enough steam.
items.fix: Make sure the sauce is at a gentle simmer before adding the eggs. If the lid is not tight, cover with foil first. Add 1 to 2 minutes of cooking time.

items.symptom: Sauce is watery around the eggs
items.cause: The tomato sauce was not reduced enough before the eggs were added.
items.fix: Continue simmering the sauce uncovered until it thickens visibly and holds a trail. A watery sauce dilutes around the eggs rather than supporting them.

content.text: Shakshuka is eaten for breakfast and brunch across North Africa and the Middle East, with particular associations with Tunisia, Israel, and Libya. The tomato and pepper combination reflects post-Columbian ingredients absorbed into the cuisine — both arrived from the Americas via the Ottoman trade routes. In Britain shakshuka became widely known through Yotam Ottolenghi's writing in the early 2010s and has settled into the weekend brunch canon alongside avocado toast and eggs Benedict. The original is faster and cheaper than either.
